reasoning_summary: The live run context for `20260421_155533_bf16_gemm_v1_83acaae4` at 26.541056 ms killed the pure occupancy thesis again, but it also exposed an important confound in the current surface. The round-7 non-PTX sibling did reach the expected machine state shift: registers dropped from 200 to 168, occupancy_limit_registers moved from 2 to 3, active warps climbed from 16.63% to 24.72%, and long scoreboard collapsed from 7.09% to 1.93%. Yet runtime regressed by 2.194943 ms because barrier exploded to 11.31%. More importantly, this was not a clean 3-CTA sibling probe: the source still carried round-6's `FixedHotBandTile128x128PtxExportScratch::kStageCount = 2`, so the non-PTX sibling also paid a 26,112 B shared-memory footprint instead of the 22,016 B class we actually wanted to compare against. That means the next best move is to deconfound the family before abandoning it: keep the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling surface but trim export scratch back to the PTX-style single stage and remeasure. Keep one same-surface barrier/handoff retime behind that, and keep the 256x128 half-panel family in third place because the user guidance still points there even though the latest clean-loop evidence does not justify reopening it immediately.
family_audit: Tiling: keep 64x64 warp tiling accepted and keep 256x128 queued, but do not reopen 256x128 first. The immediate evidence gap is now a confounded 128x128 sibling surface, not a lack of another 256x128 attempt.
Coalescing Access: keep as a guardrail. The current regression is dominated by synchronization and shared/export lifetime, not by an obvious new global-memory packing failure.
Data Reuse: accept as already present. The non-PTX sibling already reuses A and B through shared memory; the next ranking is about export scratch size and barrier cadence.
Async Copy: accept strongly. The copy path itself is not the main problem; the barrier tax around consuming the prefetched tiles is.
Bank Conflict: defer. Nothing in the latest stall breakdown points to bank conflict ahead of barrier or export lifetime.
L2 Cache: defer. Round 7 already improved DRAM and L2 movement, but runtime still regressed because synchronization exploded.

Register Reuse: accept, but mark the occupancy-only interpretation as incomplete. Registers and active warps improved exactly as intended, yet the coupled export scratch footprint and barrier cost erased the benefit.
Pg2s: accept as infrastructure already in place. The next move should not widen the pipeline; it should make the existing grouped-row sibling cheaper to synchronize.
Ps2r: accept, but map it to export-scratch and barrier cleanup on the current surface instead of another immediate family pivot.
Stage: accept, but explicitly reject the current two-stage export scratch on the non-PTX sibling. The latest run proved that stage growth plus 3-CTA residency is not a clean comparison.
Promote export-scratch trim on the grouped-row non-PTX sibling to rank 1 because it is the narrowest way to remove the current confound before deciding whether the family is really dead.
Keep a same-surface barrier/handoff retime at rank 2 because round 7 still clearly shows a synchronization wall even after registers and occupancy moved in the right direction.

Keep the 256x128 half-panel family at rank 3 because the human tiling guidance still matters, but the current loop should not abandon the accepted-base family until this export-scratch confound is resolved.

directions.name: Trim The Grouped-Row 128x128 Sibling Export Scratch To Single Stage
directions.hypothesis: Round 7 was not a clean test of 3-CTA residency on the non-PTX sibling because the source still carried a two-stage export scratch lifetime from round 6. That inflated the sibling to 26,112 B shared memory per block while barrier rose to 11.31%. The cleanest next move is to keep the same grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling surface and the same 3-CTA residency target, but trim export scratch back to a single stage so the loop can see whether the barrier tax came from occupancy itself or from the coupled scratch growth.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared export lifetime and barrier tax on the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling, currently confounded with the 3-CTA residency probe.
directions.risk: Low-moderate. This is a narrow rollback of one coupled shared/export choice on an otherwise already-correct surface.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: This is the highest-information next move because it separates the 3-CTA thesis from the accidentally coupled two-stage export scratch change.
It stays on the accepted-base family and removes one clear source of extra shared memory before the loop spends another round on a broader pivot.
The historical repo already contains a close precedent for this exact export-scratch trim, so the implementation should stay bounded.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory does not fall back toward the accepted-base class or if barrier remains in the 10%+ band after the scratch trim.
expected_local_changes: Keep the grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling launch path and 3-CTA residency target.
Trim export scratch from the current two-stage lifetime back to the PTX-style single stage.

directions.name: Retime The Non-PTX 3-CTA Barrier/Handoff Seam
directions.hypothesis: If the export-scratch trim is not enough by itself, the next same-surface move is to retime the future-tile refill order on the non-PTX sibling. The current run already proved that registers and occupancy can move in the right direction; what remains is the barrier wall. Reordering the refill seam without changing tile shape or launch bounds is the cleanest next synchronization-only variant on this surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence at the seam between cp.async wait completion, __syncthreads(), and future-tile refill ordering on the non-PTX 3-CTA sibling.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is a small scheduler-seam edit, but it still depends on codegen and can easily move stalls without improving runtime.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: Keep this same-surface retime alive because the current run still presents a textbook barrier-dominated failure mode after occupancy improved.
Rank it below dir_01 because the export-scratch confound is even cheaper to remove and makes the round-7 evidence cleaner.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if barrier does not improve materially or if runtime stays slower than the accepted base after the seam retime.
expected_local_changes: Keep the current grouped-row non-PTX 128x128 sibling surface.
Retune the future-stage refill order around the barrier seam without changing launch bounds, tile shape, or export scratch depth.

directions.name: Reopen The 256x128 Half-Panel Register-Reuse Branch Later
directions.hypothesis: The 256x128 family should stay queued because it remains the closest match to the user's explicit tiling guidance and still has the largest theoretical register-budget upside. But the loop should not jump back there until the accepted-base family has been deconfounded. Right now the higher-value information comes from learning whether the non-PTX sibling can work once its export scratch and barrier seam are cleaned up.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register reuse, B-fragment lifetime, and writer-ownership constraints on the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot.
directions.risk: High. This family remains broad and already consumed two clean-loop rounds without producing a viable surface.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: Keep this family alive because it still best matches the human 256x128 guidance.
Rank it third because the current accepted-base family still contains unresolved confounds that are cheaper to resolve first.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately on another 28-30 ms regression or any correctness failure; do not spend consecutive rounds here without a new machine-state change.
expected_local_changes: Keep the correctness-safe 256x128 writer ownership path queued.
Only reopen this family after the accepted-base 128x128 surface has been remeasured without the current export-scratch confound.

notes: Human guidance review for round 8: the current loop still respects the user's 256x128/64x64 preference, but the latest negative result is not yet a clean reason to abandon the accepted-base family. The round-7 surface carried both 3-CTA residency and a two-stage export scratch lifetime, so the first priority is to separate those effects. That is why dir_01 trims export scratch first, dir_02 keeps a same-surface barrier retime in reserve, and the 256x128 family stays queued rather than selected immediately.
